segments.text: Live session. This is a public hearing for the
Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill, and we'd love to hear from you.
We've got 10 minutes of time with you. That includes time for questions.
It's up to you how do you utilize that, and it's over to you.
Thank you. I'm here representing contractors in horticulture,
wine grapes, post-harvest, and processors.
Our organization is a voluntary organization.
Our members employ
upward of 20,000 people.
Our work is seasonal, as you can guess, and there
are two distinguishing features about our
members' workforce.
Mostly through the year, they have a small number of permanents, and depending on
the size of the business, that might be 10, 20, 30,
40 permanents. But during the seasons,
when harvest and pruning happens, that can grow to 400,
500 plus people.
And the other distinguishing feature is our workers are out in the
field. It's in
small locations, so you might have at
20 locations teams working, but they're all working in small
numbers.
And for every, say, 12 people, there's a
supervisor. So highly supervised, small teams
working around the countryside.
What the bill focuses on is the number of employees.
Our submission is the number of employees doesn't actually determine risk.
It can be a factor, but the risk is determined about where you're working.
So our ask is to recognize our special
circumstances in the bill and change that
number quotient to either be an average across the year, but
also to recognize that we work at small
sites,
where the workers might number less than 20 under direct
supervision. And so,
our real view is that if the bill could be amended to make allowance
for contracting. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Mike. Just to
start us off,
that is something we have heard frequently, so we do appreciate a different
perspective on how that could be framed.
So, the first question we have is from Jan Tinetti.
Hi, Mike. Good to see you.
Hi, Jan.
In a new role, well, different role from when I probably last spoke to you.
I've-
Yeah. Well, I float around.
Hey, I've got a couple of questions about that threshold, and-
Yes
... I'm just trying to understand from this perspective, can
you sort of talk me through probably the modeling or
evidence that you've got to suggest you talk about recommending
raising it to, I think it was 40 or 50 workers in-
Yeah
... your submission. So what would be the modeling in that that you've used to
determine that figure?
Just roughly our membership size.
But,
having thought about it,
I think actually having that number i- in the bill as
a sort of prerequisite, it misses the point around the
risk. The risk isn't about the numbers, the risk is about the direct
supervision. And I think the bill will become a lot stronger if it focused
on, is the group being properly supervised?
And you might have a ratio of, say, there must be,
if you want to take advantage of these provisions, one supervisor
for 12 workers,
and also require that they work at different sites.
So it's not one big site where 200 people are working,
it's a lot of small sites. So my submission is, as moves a
little bit, I'd actually take away the number and-
Right
... put it into direct supervision, because that makes a lot more sense.
Yeah.
You can just see the WorkSafe inspector coming along, going, "You've got 21."
You know what I mean?
Yeah. We've, I think, heard that quite a bit throughout the submissions.
Hey, on that then,
and this is
something I want to get my head around, too, you're a
oversight organization. So if that
were increased or changed-
Yes
... what would you expect your members to voluntarily
maintain to ensure
safety standards remain high? So there must be something that you have thought
about in that space.
So,
as you appreciate, all the business in New Zealand have really high standards now
because of the current legislation.
Yeah.
Our expectation when it comes to health and safety,
work safety,
is to meet the, not only the minimum New Zealand legal standard,
but to go further in terms of worker care.
Our name is quite lofty, Ethical Employers.
Mm.
Our real focus is in
employment and best
practice employment for the workers so that we're not
exploiting. That's our real drive.
But part of that, of course, is keeping the workers safe.
And so we expect our employers, and we do audit, to
go beyond what the minimum legal requirements are.
And we've got no problem with providing WorkSafe with information or anything
that they want,
because what we're trying to do is lift the performance of all
contractors in the country.
Yeah. Oh, look, that's super helpful. Thanks, Mike.
Thank you, Mike. It doesn't appear that we have any further questions, but as I
mentioned earlier, it's a very topical point that you make, so we really
appreciate your contribution.
Thank you very much for hearing me.
Thank you.
Thank you.
Okay. If we have now, APEX and NZRDA
Unions available online, we'll move next to them.
Welcome, Natalia de Souza. We've 10 minutes for your
submission, and that includes time for questions.
Thank you. Nga mihi nui kia koutou.
Thank you for the opportunity to represent our submission to the
Select Committee. As mentioned, I am representing both
APEX and the RDA Unions, and collectively, we represent
over 8,500, close to 9,000 employees in the health
sector.And that spans allied health and resident
doctors employed in both public and private sectors.
Having taken our submission as read, there are two things we'd like to briefly
emphasize here. First, we think the bill is wrongly
prioritizing the reduction of compliance costs over the
meaningful reduction of harm, injuries, and fatalities.
In high-stakes environment like healthcare, these costs of
complying with legislation are actually essential investments
that keep our workers safe, healthy, and able to continue
the quite literally life-saving work they do.
So obviously this has natural flow on implications then for things like patient
safety.
And second, and importantly, we see the most dangerous part of this
bill being the narrow defined list of critical
risks which exclude musculoskeletal injuries and
psychosocial hazards, which of course, can give rise to things like
workplace violence and bullying, slips, trips and falls,
and even fatigue. And all of these are, unfortunately, commonplace
and an everyday reality for many healthcare workers.
Our main point is that failing to prevent these risks doesn't actually save any
money, it's simply shifting the cost, which we estimate at billions
if we look at Australian statistics.
Shifting those costs onto the public health system, ACC, and the
taxpayer ultimately. The bottom line is health and safety is
part and parcel of a good business model, and we shouldn't be
incentivizing poor business practices, which have proven to increase
workplace harm, increase cost to the economy, and reduce
productivity,
to what end?
Thank you, and I'm open to taking any questions.
Wonderful. Thank you, Natalia. It doesn't appear that we have any questions from...
Oh, we do. We have a question from Jan Tinizzi.
Sorry, Natalia. I was just
trying to find the raise hand function.
But can you sort of delve into that?
You talked about the ACC, and that's something that
many submitters have actually come before and talked about the increased
risks. Do you see for your organization and your members
that that is probably,
like that's quite big with the risks
and that that they're facing in their work
throughout that time, and do you see that there's an integral
part of this bill... Well, this is what I'm trying to get my head around.
So I'm not trying to lead, and I'm happy for you to come and say no,
but is there a real integral part of
ACC there that the bill is not covering at the moment?
Yeah, look, thank you for that question.
It's really an interesting one in healthcare in particular.
So I mentioned it in the submission, but I can talk to here that probably the
three most common big psychosocial risks we see in health
are things like musculoskeletal injuries, so particularly for our
sonographers who are doing ultrasounds, but also our radiographers.
Things like workplace stress and fatigue
from high workloads and understaffing.
And then of course, things like workplace violence and bullying, both from patients
and their whanau, but also from other employees as well.
So if we take any one of those examples, even something like sonography,
where, I can give you an example where we had a sonographer who was
starting to
get really achy and starting to have a lot of wrist and shoulder pain
because of the repetitive scanning.
Even something like going in there and saying, "Hey, how about
varying scanning patterns?" So a really small shift so that they're not doing the
same scan over and over again can reduce some of those injuries.
Now, if that were to escalate, obviously you'd see costs through ACC because
it's a workplace injury claim. Similar with, we've seen
countless cases of people who have experienced workplace bullying and
harassment, and where that hasn't been resolved, and they've been forced to
take time off or even leave eventually.
Again, many of them don't feel ready or immediately ready to return
to work. And so there's ACC costs there in terms of actually
supporting their
return to work in terms of their stress to the point where they may not even be
able to return to work. So there's a number of examples of how these
costs are simply just externalized through other agencies.
Even things like bullying, where if we can't prevent it through health and safety
legislation, it will simply end up in the ERA, in the Employment Relations
Authority or courts and clog up those systems even further.
So the real sort of superpower, I suppose, of the health and safety
legislation is to actually try and prevent a lot of those risks
from compounding, from escalating before they come with those greater
costs.
That's really interesting, and it's the unintended consequences that,
I guess, I'm trying to get to, and as a committee, we're trying to get to the
bottom of so that we don't allow that to happen.
But I guess I've got a question then.
In the current legislation, what's not working in the current
legislation now, in your opinion, for your members?
Or what
could go further in that legislation to make it even tighter for them?
I mean, look, there's probably quite a lot.
I'd say one is, regardless, we'd need to strengthen our
focus on psychosocial risk and minimization and prevention.
But I think the other really important lever that already exists in the
legislation is things like worker participation.
And what we find is across public sector, they tend to be a little more in
place. There tends to be policies and practices around it, but not so
much in private sector and smaller employers.
And so if we can try and find ways of increasing that
key worker participation function, then it'll be really important.
I mean, studies have shown that where we see greater worker participation,
we see better health and safety outcomes.
So just involving the employees on the ground, they're probably, in a lot of times,
really best placed to actually know what's the most dangerous or risky part of
their job, or the part of the job that's really causing a lot of concerns.
And having their involvement or voice in it is invaluable.
Madam Chair, can I ask another question on that?
Sure.
Thank you. So the ACOTS in this bill then
looks like that they're bringing that in, and I think that's a part of the bill
that most of us can agree is quite a good part.
Do you think that goes far enough
for that worker participation, or do you think that there needs to be,
particularly from your members' point of view, another way of doing that?
Yeah, look, I think it's more about how we actually put worker participation into
practice. So that's one part of it.
Now, I know that initially, when the bill was introduced, worker
participation was only required in organizations that had, say, more than 20
employees, and now that's gone away. So that's really helpful, that's fantastic.
More guidance from the regulator around how employers can actually put that
into place would be excellent. The ACOTS themselves, look, they're a really
valuable introduction in this bill, particularly the
ability for other representatives, so whether that's employee or employer
representatives, to have input into developing and shaping that, so that's
fantastic. However, we do see risks of actually using that as
a, "Well, we complied with our duties," in court kind of
thing. So what we argue is that it should be a floor rather than
a ceiling, and there's lots of room to actually make improvements on
approved codes of practice, and try and keep up with what
the current risks are as well, because, of course, those codes won't always be
super current or relevant.
Oh, look, that's super helpful. Thank you very much.
Thank you, Natalia.
Thank you.
I really appreciate your contribution to this process.
And we'll move on now to our next-
Thank you.
Thank you.
Right. We now have Physiotherapy New Zealand
joining us.
We have Kirsten Davey and Simon Hoare.
We'd love to see your faces.
Oh.
Wonderful. Good morning.
There we go.
Yeah. We have 10 minutes with you this morning, and we're running a little ahead of
time, so thank you for being agile.
And that does include time for questions.
That's over to you how you utilize the time, and you can start now.
Brilliant. Thank you very much. And just wanted to... Can you hear me okay?
Wonderfully.
Perfect. Great. Well, thank you very much for allowing us the opportunity to
speak. We are presenting on behalf of Physiotherapy New Zealand, and in
particular, our occupational health special interest group.
My name is Simon Hoare, I'm the CEO.
Hoare. Kirsten Davey, I'm
president of Physiotherapy New Zealand. Nice to be here.
Thank you for this opportunity.
Great.
So look, physiotherapists daily deal with the effects of
workplace injuries, and in particular musculoskeletal injuries,
musculoskeletal conditions. So physiotherapists work
in the workplace and clinics with employers
and employees to mitigate the significant cost and impact
of musculoskeletal harm on businesses, on individuals,
and on society as well. So I'll just hand over to Kirsten now to talk a little bit
more in detail about that.
So I think picking up and emphasizing the main points that are in
our submission. We do have concerns around that
critical risks, that terminology, that how the bill narrows that
focus in a way that sort of risks worsening New Zealand's
largest source of workplace harm, which is those
musculoskeletal conditions, which is, of course,
who physiotherapists are dealing with on a daily
basis. So shifting that attention toward critical
risk sort of inherently implies workplaces can
place less attention on minor injuries,
which the musculoskeletal injuries tend to fall
into. And those are workers who are undertaking manual
handling, repetitive tasks, sustained physical load.
They contribute to musculoskeletal injuries, those
sprains, strains, back injuries.
And yet musculoskeletal harm is not minor.
And if we look at some of the ACC costs, musculoskeletal
injuries make up 75% of
ACC work injury costs. So it's the dominant
and growing cost, placing growing burdens on the individual, so on the
worker, which we're really concerned about, on our
funders, and on employers and productivity, and
therefore New Zealand as a whole.
We're concerned that this risk will continue to escalate with significant
long-term harm consequences for workers, and that health
system generally.
So our thoughts are that the definition of critical risk
becomes risky, particularly for small PCBUs.
And note the prescriptive definition is no longer current
practice and has been sort of abandoned years ago by
Australia and the UK, whose rates of harm in the
workplace for musculoskeletal conditions is certainly much lower.
We also note that other areas of significant risk,
such as psychosocial hazards, may not be captured in
that critical risks area. And that's, again, another area
that physiotherapists work in. Mental health is another huge
area, just like your previous presenter was talking
about.I'll hand back to Simon to-
Yeah. So I suppose, we're trying to keep it brief.
So, that proposed shift risks, in our view, weakening the
regulatory oversight in a time when musculoskeletal harm
already lacks a bit of visibility.
So that increased emphasis on guidance and reduced enforcement makes it
difficult to see how this will make businesses take this seriously, and
obviously health and safety is very, very important.
And so our members, so members of Physiotherapy New Zealand, already have some
concerns after the disbandment of WorkSafe's Human Factors and
Ergonomics team, which had a focus on
musculoskeletal harm.
So I think what we're trying to highlight is, I think the
proposed bill risks compromising some of the objectives by
prioritizing only critical risks.
And we'd like to see sort of that broader review alongside ACC,
alongside health, alongside social welfare and MB
to assess those impacts. I think we all know that we're really lucky to have
this legislation to protect workers.
And so we want to ensure that any changes don't have unintended
consequences by narrowing that focus to just critical risks,
when we know that musculoskeletal harm and musculoskeletal injuries are
the sort of largest workforce harm area.
So we want to make sure that we protect the workers, the businesses, and
New Zealand as a whole.
We'll open up to questions. And Jan, see that you've got your hand...
Ooh, we've got a couple of hands up there.
Yes, we have. So look,
if you don't mind, or just we like to go based on who
puts their hands up first.
So Jan will go lead off, and then we'll have Dr. Panjit Palmer.
Thank you, and thank you for your submission.
Now, I'm looking over this way because I've got your submission on another screen
here. And in your submission, you do express
concern about the proposed shift towards greater
guidance and less enforcement. I've got two questions
based on that, if that's okay with Madam Chair, with that.
From your experience working with injured workers and
workplaces, how important,
in your experience, is the regulatory enforcement in driving
meaningful prevention of musculoskeletal harm?
I'll answer that one, because I do work out in,
with employers, so I'm well-placed.
Look, I think the majority of New Zealand employers
are
trying to do a good job for their employees.
They are aware of their legislative
responsibilities, but there can be
confusion, and it is important that the legislation is
streamlined and this sort of distinction between
critical risk and
orientation towards that could create more confusion, especially for those
smaller organizations. And we know New Zealand is made up 90%
of small organizations. And physiotherapy practices
alike, and I'm a physiotherapy practice owner.
So,
the will and intent is there. I think having
legislation that's streamlined, that's clear,
that
gives good guidance is important.
Thank you.
That is how I find employers.
Do I have time, Madam Chair?
If it's quick, yes.
Thank you. So you-
Where's the question?
In the same part you refer to the disbandment of WorkSafe's Human Factors
and Ergonomics team.
Can you give me an impact on if this has had an impact on the
ability of the regulators to identify high-risk
musculoskeletal environments or systemic workplace issues?
Well, certainly our members who were involved
have seen a change in that ability
for
really employers having that focus on
that manual handling, repetitive task.
Just that
awareness, that streamline, they have noticed, our
occupational health group have noticed a difference.
And that's why we've called that out in our submission, that it's
not as clear. Having
the more information and guidance that can be given,
the better. So when you have that stepping back of those sorts of committees,
that just becomes challenging,
and has an impact at the workforce,
at the coal face. Simon, you might-
Yeah. Look, what I was going to say is, as you know, all different businesses
function in all different ways, and so you need that sort of portfolio of different
things to tackle different parts of it.
And so when you start losing some of the focus in the legislation, you start losing
some of those teams. I think the risk is you start to create an
opportunity for things to get missed by businesses.
So I think just that overall weakening, the sort of shift in focus
is what we're concerned about.
Thank you.
Right. Can I move on now to the question we have from Dr. Panjit Palmer?
Thank you. So first I want to say thank you to you for sharing your
concerns based on your professional experience.
And can I, my overall question is about understanding then
how do we approach the main objective here.
So the main objective is to kind of not go with that all-risk
approach, because we want to balance it with the need of compliance
on small PCBUs, because small PCBUs have limited
resources and capacity. So how do we, if we
include the concerns that you have expressed in the legislation,
then how do we ensure that we are able to manage that as well, balancing
the all-risk approach versus the compliance requirement from small
businesses kind of thing?
And look, as a small business owner, I understand and applaud
your intent here around trying to reduce
compliance. But,
I-The concern then
becomes that we then lose sight of
a big risk. Just because it's not a critical risk doesn't mean it's not
a big risk. Critical risk in looking at people that are going
to suffer serious harm. But we also know that a whole lot of
small missed smaller
injuries can indicate that there
could be a more major injury about to occur, and
that's clear. So missing those smaller injuries, not taking
so much notice of them, not having a focus on them, I think,
does leave the worker, and more importantly, the employees
exposed. So how do we do that? Well, from
our point of view, MSK injuries and psychosocial are both
really important, and they mightn't fall under your definition.
Does it reduce compliance costs by not focusing on those
from an employer's point of view? From my point of view, I'm not sure that it
does. So,
yeah. Simon, I don't know.
Thank you. Look, that's unfortunately all the time we've got with you this morning.
Oh, okay. All right. Thank you.
Thank you very much. We'll move on now.
We appreciate your contribution to this process.
And thank you for the opportunity.
Thank you.
Really appreciate it.
Wonderful.
We now have MINEX Health and Safety Council, Wayne
Scott.
Morning, Wayne. Welcome to the Select Committee and the hearing on the
Health and Safety at Work Amendment Bill.
We have 10 minutes with you.
You seem awfully far away, but that's the way your camera's set up, so hopefully we
can hear you, and that 10 minutes includes time for questions.
We'll pass it over to you.
Sorry about the set up of the camera.
I couldn't find a 12-year-old to help me this morning.
That's okay, we can hear you well. We can still see you.
Thanks. Thank you for your time today. I'm Wayne Scott.
I'm the Chief Executive of MINEX. We're the Health and Safety Council for the
extractive sector, which covers mining, quarrying and tunneling.
You read our submission, so I just wanted to pick up on a couple of points and
expand on those.
Firstly, while we support the core objective of the Bill, to strengthen
organizational focus on the workplace risks most capable of causing
death or serious harm, the inclusion of Schedule 5 from the Mining
and Quarrying Regulations in Schedule 1A of the Bill may
lead to sites focusing on this list of risks rather than determining the
fatal hazards within their own businesses.
Schedule 5 was introduced to the Mining and Quarrying Regulations as a list of
notifiable events, not as an assessment of critical risks in the
sector.
Our sector is very familiar with the concept of fatal hazards and the use of
critical control management systems to identify critical controls,
define performance criteria, and verify that controls are effective.
Our experience across large and small businesses is that serious incidents
rarely occur because hazards were unknown.
They occur when the controls intended to manage those hazards failed.
There is a danger that the current wording of the Bill encourages businesses to
focus solely on identifying critical risks and not on controlling
them effectively. Knowing your critical risks is not enough.
Businesses need to identify, maintain, and verify the controls that
are critical to preventing fatal or life-altering harm.
Another concern we have is that the proposed critical risk definition does not
consider that there is potential of fatal harm to workers from latent
hazards such as fatigue, exposure to severe weather, and other
workplace psychosocial hazards.
WorkSafe New Zealand determined in a review of suicides from 2017 to
2021 that 12% of suicides met the criteria of being
work-related.
These hazards can have a cumulative effect on workers over time, leading to
accidents and incidents that cause harm.
Under the Health and Safety at Work Act, health includes both
physical and mental health, and PCBUs must manage
psychosocial risks in the same way as any other serious risk.
We are concerned that under these changes, PCBUs will not need to manage
these hazards, and workers will be seriously harmed over time as a result.
We strongly support the greater use of codes of practice as our sector has
benefited from both our own regulations and significant
guidance, both from WorkSafe and our industry associations.
These documents are resource-intensive to develop, but deliver real
benefits in providing clarity to PCBUs.
It is important that WorkSafe is resourced to develop these documents in a timely
manner, and that simple processes are developed for WorkSafe endorsement of
industry-led codes of practice. I'm happy to take questions.
Wonderful. Thank you. It appears we have a question from Jan Tinetti.
Kia ora, and thanks, Wayne. I was particularly interested in this submission
because, of course,
you've got such high-risk factors within the industry,
and I really appreciate those areas that you've
identified. But looking at your submission, I wanted to ask
a couple of questions around the small business carve-out that you've
mentioned in your submission as well.
Yep.
You
argue that the small business carve-out is unworkable where
multiple PCBUs operate together.
Have you got concrete examples of that within mining or quarrying,
where differing duties would create gaps or conflicts in
risk control?
Yeah, we have, particularly some of the larger mining sites where there could be
multiple contractors on site.
We've also had it on some of our
smaller quarry sites where tyre fitters will come on site
or there'll be other PCBUs operating on site,
and there has often been-Should we say misunderstandings between
what's required
of the different PCBUs and who's responsible for what.
We have got some clarity within our
regulations. So we're one of the sectors that's lucky to have our own regulations,
so there is a little bit of clarity there.
Yeah.
But our concern is that these changes, particularly when you
take an arbitrary number and say 19's small and 21's
large, I just think some of those things could create some
unintended consequences where you have multiple PCBUs on
sites.
Thank you. And just on the small PCBUs, you did note in your
submission that small PCBUs experience higher
injury rates, and just how we can make that stronger in
the legislation. Do you attribute that primarily to capability
and resourcing or regulatory settings?
And how would your proposed amendments address the root causes?
I don't think it's so much the
regulatory settings. I think it's the ability of organizations
at a certain scale to understand what's expected of them.
And I think the minister has talked about that a bit in relation to this bill.
Yeah.
I don't know, though, that these changes are actually going to do that.
What we found
with our regulations that were post-Pipe River, you know, in 2015-
Yeah
... that the greater degree of clarity really helped those
small businesses,
and in fact, at the time, they were a little bit concerned about
the greater regulatory burden, but in fact, they've benefited from that.
And I think if we asked those small businesses now,
they would say they've really benefited from having that greater direction.
Oh, that's look super helpful from my perspective. Thank you.
Thank you very much, Wayne. We do appreciate your contribution to this process.
And very thoughtful and fulsome submission.
Thank you very much.
Thank you.
And we'll move on now to the Hazels Miller Foundation.
We have Anne Miller and Mike again with us.
Mike O'Brien.
Good morning. And do we have-- Oh, we do have Mike O'Brien on the call
with us.
Yes.
Just wait for the speaker.
Get you to turn your camera on. Wonderful. There we go.
Try that. Hopefully that's much better.
Beautiful. That's okay. Hi again, Mike.
You are with us for 10 minutes again this morning, and that includes time for
questions, and the time is over to you now.
Wonderful. Thank you very much, Madam Chair.
And I come to you with a different hat on today, and that is
of the Hazels Miller Foundation. We had
hoped that we could get one of our fellow trustees, Gregor Costa,
to attend, but unfortunately, he was unavailable this morning.
And so I'm stepping in to support Anne Miller, who is a fellow
trustee, who will speak on behalf of the Hazels Miller Foundation.
My role is as chair of the foundation.
We're an
independent charitable trust established in 2024 with the
support of the Miller family. Our purpose is public
benefit, not representation.
We don't advocate for employers, workers, or professional
members. Our focus is on education and training for people working
in, on entering, and the health and safety sector,
building workforce capability and capacity, and improving public
health and safety outcomes through consistent evidence-based
practice. Our goal
from a fundraising perspective, is to raise sufficient funds
to put in substantive scholarships and educational
capability
over the next 100 years. And we're on a
pathway to raise to approximately $5 million, which will give
us the ability to put 30 to 50 scholarships in-market and
raise the capability of the workforce
for every year for 100 years. So that's our goal.
And so we're trying to really make a difference to education.
I'll hand over to Anne.
Thanks. Thank you, and
dear members,
for this opportunity.
It would help if I explain that
we
gave the money because my brother was into occupational hygiene,
and was
a visionary, and he used to tell me what he was seeing New Zealand would
be like in 30 years' time. And his vision was for New Zealand to
be self-sufficient in health and safety, that you didn't need to go
to Britain for the qualifications, or America, or anywhere
else.
And part of the reason that as soon as we knew he had passed, that was what we
decided we wanted something to be a lasting
effect because he loved New Zealand.
He had been here 17 years or something like that.
And lots of people knew him because he was kind of famous.
So that's kind of the background of where we're coming from.
We were brought up in hospitality, so health and safety was part and
parcel of our remit, because
hospitality, your food poisoning,
falls, you name it, it happens.
So that's the background. So what mattered to him was people
having the knowledge and capability to keep themselves and others safe.
Decisions being grounded in evidence and sound practice.
Systems that encourage learning, ongoing improvements,
and lasting results instead of focusing on compliance.
He was very much into teaching and getting people to have that education.
The Miller Foundation exists to carry those values forward.
That is why we focus on education, workforce development, and building

Yeah.

If we could fix that, return to the emphasis of approved codes of

and include the safe work instruments where they are not appropriate,

I think we've all got all the tools already.

Thanks, Ken. That's very helpful.

Analogous to a crucible, and the function is to take evidence,

Proposing a carve-out will lead to operational creep in the function of the law,

basically, the same all the way through.

our industry, whilst the DW covers manufacturing workers and

Yes. We believe it's not achieving that clarity, and also we

critical
matters and

It's an absolute holistic approach, and this approach unpicks that

Thank you.
Thank you very much to the both of you for joining us and contributing to this

Thank you.
Thank you.
Thank you. Committee, we'll move into our break time
